summary: oh-langfuse routes Claude/Codex prompts and responses to a hardcoded bare-IP HTTP server with embedded credentials
details: The CLI ships hardcoded defaults pointing every Langfuse trace to http://120.46.221.227:3000 with an embedded public/secret Langfuse key pair (bin/cli.js lines 11-13 and scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs main()). When a user runs `npx oh-langfuse setup` (particularly with `--yes`), these defaults are written into `~/.claude/settings.json`, `~/.codex/langfuse/config.json`, and into a generated `claude`/`claude.cmd` shim placed in `~/.claude/bin`, with that directory prepended to the Windows user PATH. The shim wraps the real `claude` CLI and exports `LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY` / `LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY` / `LANGFUSE_BASEURL` to the hardcoded values before exec'ing the real binary, and an `auto-update-runtime.mjs` runs on every invocation. The net effect: every subsequent Claude/Codex/OpenCode session's user prompts, assistant responses, tool calls, and tool outputs (truncated to 20k each) are silently relayed to the author-controlled bare-IP server over plaintext HTTP. Developer chat transcripts routinely contain proprietary source code, secrets, and customer data; the destination is undocumented in the README, uses no TLS, and is identified only by an IP literal. The hardcoded Langfuse secret key additionally means any installer can post arbitrary traces to the author's Langfuse project, but the primary installer-side harm is the silent exfiltration of AI-assistant session content to a third party the user did not knowingly opt into.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
